description:
## Problem

`runtime/src/scheduler_hooks.rs` stores `SPAWN_HOOK`, `SPAWN_OPT_HOOK`, and `SEND_HOOK` as process-global `AtomicUsize` slots. A `Runtime` is single-worker by design and installs/clears these around `run_until_idle` (`src/runtime.rs:286-380`). With `cargo test` running tests on multiple threads in the same process, two Runtimes can interleave:

- Test A: `install(A)` → starts driving tasks
- Test B: `install(B)` → `run_until_idle` → `clear()`
- Test A's still-live task calls `fz_spawn` → loads 0 → panics at `scheduler_hooks.rs:90`

Because the FFI entry points are `extern "C"`, the panic is `panic_nounwind` and aborts the whole test process. Observed flake:

```
thread 'runtime::tests::fixture_ping_pong_via_jit_runtime' panicked at runtime/src/scheduler_hooks.rs:90:9:
fz_spawn called outside a Runtime — install_spawn_hook must be called before driving any task
thread caused non-unwinding panic. aborting.
```

## Why globals were chosen, and why that no longer applies

`scheduler_hooks.rs:50-58` explains the choice: an earlier `thread_local!` attempt produced multiple `__ZN…SPAWN_HOOK…_tlv\$init` symbols with different hashes in AOT-linked binaries, so install wrote to one slot and dispatch read from another. The fix was to use a single global.

That symptom is the fingerprint of TLS defined in the **binary** crate being seen from the runtime staticlib through the extern "C" boundary — two object files get their own copy of the slot. fz-ul4.23.10 then moved `scheduler_hooks` into the runtime crate (file header, lines 8-10). The runtime crate already exposes thread-locals to AOT-linked binaries today — `CURRENT_PROCESS` (`runtime/src/process.rs`) and `CURRENT_RUNTIME` (`src/runtime.rs:67`) both work fine across the staticlib boundary. The original root cause was dissolved by the .23.10 move; the `AtomicUsize` is now a workaround for a problem that no longer exists, and it actively breaks the data model.

## Data-model argument

A `Runtime` is single-threaded by design (fz-ul4.19.1). \"The current Runtime\" is naturally a per-thread concept. The runtime already uses `thread_local!` for `CURRENT_RUNTIME` and `CURRENT_PROCESS`, saved/restored around `run_until_idle`. The hook slots are the only piece that pretends to be process-wide. Making them thread-local restores the parallel-Runtimes-per-thread property the rest of the design already assumes.

## Plan

1. In `runtime/src/scheduler_hooks.rs`, replace each `static …: AtomicUsize` with a `thread_local! { static …: Cell<usize> }`. Update `install_*`, `clear_*`, and `dispatch_*` accordingly. Keep the existing panic guard so a `fz_spawn` from a thread that never installed hooks still aborts loudly.
2. Drop the AtomicUsize-rationale comment; replace with a short note on per-thread semantics and a pointer to `CURRENT_PROCESS` as the existence proof that runtime-crate TLS works under AOT.
3. Verify AOT linkage and dispatch through the new slot by running the AOT fixtures (`cargo test --workspace`, which exercises both JIT and AOT paths) and at least one explicit AOT fixture script.
4. Verify the race is gone by running the previously-flaky test under load (e.g. `for i in {1..50}; do cargo test -p fz runtime::tests::fixture_ping_pong_via_jit_runtime --quiet || break; done`).

## Acceptance

- All hook storage is thread-local, defined in `runtime/src/scheduler_hooks.rs`.
- `cargo test --workspace` is green.
- The flaky test passes under repeated parallel runs (no abort).
- AOT fixtures still link and run.
- A `fz_spawn` from a thread without an installed hook still panics with the existing message.

## Out of scope

- Multi-worker Runtime / work-stealing. This ticket preserves the single-worker model; it just stops two single-worker Runtimes on two threads from clobbering each other.
title: scheduler hooks: thread-local slots so parallel Runtimes don't race
